sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient, a 30-year-old male, presents with a several-week history of right shoulder pain that began after a period of intense physical activity, including yard work and lifting heavy objects.
He describes the pain as persistent and worsening with movement, particularly on the outside of the shoulder.
He reports tenderness to palpation on the outside of the shoulder, but no restriction in range of motion.
He denies numbness or weakness in the arm or hand.
He has a history of similar pain in the same shoulder from a previous injury playing baseball.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of a previous injury to the right shoulder from playing baseball, which resulted in some ongoing issues.
He does not have any other significant medical history.
He denies any smoking, alcohol, or drug use.
He is not sure if he drinks enough water, as his wife has mentioned this to him.
Review of systems: The patient reports some stiffness in both feet and ankles, which he attributes to recent yard work.
He has been using ice and elevation to help alleviate the symptoms.
Current medications: The patient is taking Tylenol (acetaminophen) as needed for pain, and has been using ice and Epsom salts to help with his foot and ankle pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure is 116/84, heart rate is 62, respirations are 16, temperature is 97.8, and oxygen saturation is 99%.
Physical exam: The patient has limited active and passive range of motion in the right shoulder, with tenderness at the greater tuberosity of the humerus.
There is no tenderness at the sternoclavicular or acromioclavicular joints.
The patient has a good hand grip, and on neurovascular exam of the right arm, capillary refill is less than three seconds and sensation to touch is intact.
Test Results: The patient's shoulder X-ray shows no fracture.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's symptoms are most likely due to rotator cuff tendinopathy, likely caused by the recent intense physical activity.
Problem list: Rotator cuff tendinopathy (confirmed); Foot and ankle pain (unknown); Dehydration (suspected).
Plan:
I prescribed Tylenol as needed for pain.
I referred the patient to physical therapy for approximately six to eight weeks.
I ordered an MRI to rule out any other conditions.
I advised the patient to continue using ice and Epsom salts for his foot and ankle pain.
If the patient's symptoms do not improve with physical therapy, we may consider a steroid injection for the shoulder.
Follow-up: I will follow up with the patient in six to eight weeks to assess the progress of his physical therapy and adjust the treatment plan as needed.
